event: The Assumption of the Virgin Mary
symbols: ascending woman in white — the assumption of the Virgin into heavenly glory
crown of roses held above her — the coronation that awaits her in heaven
white robes — purity, sinlessness, and glorified state
surrounding cherubim and angels — the heavenly host receiving her
cross carried by an angel — the redemptive work of Christ that makes this glorification possible
dove descending — the Holy Spirit accompanying the assumption
golden light above — the divine presence welcoming her into heaven
upward gaze — anticipation of the beatific vision
description: A woman in flowing white robes ascends through golden clouds, her blonde hair streaming, her gaze directed upward with arms slightly open. She is surrounded by numerous cherubim and putti — plump infant angels — who fly, tumble, and hover around her. One angel at upper left holds a crown of roses above her head. Another angel at lower left carries a wooden cross. A dove descends from above. The scene is bathed in warm golden light emanating from the upper portion of the composition, with darker clouds below.
theological_description: The Assumption of the Virgin, while not directly narrated in Scripture, has deep roots in early Christian tradition and theological reflection. The scene depicts Mary being taken bodily into heaven at the end of her earthly life. The theological logic rests on Mary's unique role as Theotokos — the God-bearer — whose body, having housed the incarnate Word, was preserved from corruption. The crown of roses held by the angel anticipates her coronation as Queen of Heaven, reflecting the woman 'clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars' (Revelation 12:1), a passage traditionally interpreted as referring to Mary. The cross borne by the attendant angel reminds the viewer that Mary's glorification flows entirely from the redemptive sacrifice of her Son. The surrounding host of angels fulfills the imagery of Psalm 45:14-15, where the queen is brought to the king with joy and gladness, attended by her companions. Mary's assumption serves as a sign of hope for all believers — if the mother of the Lord is glorified, then the promise of bodily resurrection extends to all who are in Christ.

scripture_refs.reason: The woman clothed with the sun and crowned with stars, traditionally interpreted as an image of Mary glorified in heaven
The angel's greeting 'full of grace' provides the theological basis for Mary's unique glorification
The queen brought before the king in glory with her attendants, read as prefiguring Mary's heavenly reception
In Christ all shall be made alive — Mary's assumption as the first fruit of the resurrection promise to believers
Mary's Magnificat prophecy that all generations will call her blessed and the Mighty One has done great things for her
